story:
Agatha, the old woman of the forest, finds a prim, fur-cloaked child at her doorstep: Lady Beatrice the Third, golden-haired and jeweled, certain she’s found a witch. Beatrice insists she saw Agatha casting spells among the trees and demands to be taken on as an apprentice. Agatha denies it, teasing the girl with a mock curse toad or worm, only to be told, quite solemnly, that witches feed on young girls’ souls—and Beatrice would trade hers if it meant learning magic. Alarmed by what the child reads but curious about her stubbornness, Agatha asks what she really wants. Beatrice refuses to say.

Agatha returns to her chores, but the girl hounds her every step: inspecting the grain bin for hexes, peering into the henhouse as if the birds might be demons in disguise, squinting at garden tools for hidden charms. Day after day, the routine settles like weather. When ignoring fails to banish her, Agatha greets Beatrice in the mornings and, with a sigh, puts her to work. The little lady is no quick study, but she is willing. Agatha teaches her how not to startle the chickens, how to gather eggs, how to pull weeds by the roots, and which insects are foes and which are friends. The girl’s pink dresses come away brown; she never once complains. In her mind, this is “secret witch training,” and she lays verbal snares: Do witches have black cats or owls? How many make a coven? Have you ever turned someone into a frog?

As the cottage grows tidy and the chores thin out, Agatha teaches sewing, knitting, basket weaving. One morning she finds her sons’ old pastels, half-used, and offers them. Beatrice’s face brightens, then hardens into business again. She draws and chatters—until she goes quiet. Agatha leans over to find a radiant ocean: sand, gulls, sunlight glimmering like jewels. “It’s my ocean,” the girl whispers, then rushes out, leaving her gloves behind.

The next day brings no knock. Night brings the Duke, worry-lined, begging for help. The tracks lead into the forest; the rain has smothered the hounds. Agatha gathers shawl, bell-staff, blanket, and the gloves, and sends the men searching where they will not find her. She goes where the forest shows her: to the ancient tree whose roots sprawl like arms. There, under its lowered boughs, lies Beatrice, shivering in her ruined finery. Agatha wraps her, breathes warmth into her, and the girl revives with a triumphant smile. “I knew you were a witch! You used my gloves to find me—and you sent the men searching where they will not find me after the rain smothered the hounds, so you could come alone after the Duke begged at your door.” Agatha laughs and tells the truth: she found Beatrice because she knows the woods like her own hands—and because children often end up here, as she and her sons once did.

The child’s bravado crumbles. She wanted a witch to send her back to the coast, to her mother’s first home, to the ocean’s diamonds. The forest feels dark and loud. Agatha explains that fear belongs to the unknown: the towering trees cradle life; the calls are neighbors. Home by the sea will remain hers, but this place can become home too, if she learns it. And the Duke? Odd, yes. Cruel, no. He was frantic to find her. The girl, surprised, leans in and sleeps. Agatha rings her bell; the searchers come. Beatrice announces she wants the ways of the forest, not witchcraft. “Tomorrow,” Agatha says.

Back home, aching but smiling, Agatha settles by the hearth. The fire blooms at a flick of her fingers. She thinks of tonics, and of which stray spell the girl might have glimpsed. Next time, she’ll be more careful.
